feed.title: Manav Panchal
feed.subtitle: Wannabe everything. Still figuring out what I want to do. Undergrad at the University of Alberta.

entry.title: Learning Go, Day 3
entry.content:
One thing to add before we proceed, While doing the exercises in the book (1.3), I found out that ioutil.Discard has been deprecated, and now it is just io.Discard

Also, here’s a question I had when solving What happens if I don’t close resp.Body?

READ THE BOOK DAWG.


Let’s start with Chapter 1.3

Finding Duplicate Lines

This is a table to refer to for proper formatting (Standard C stuff tbh):

I’m not going to share the code here. It’s a simple program that reads from stdin and prints out the lines that are repeated. I’m going to share the code for the next exercise, which is a bit more interesting.

GIFs

Go has a standard image package. We got to make Lissajous figures. Code will be posted in the repo.

Fetching URLs Concurrently

ioutil.ReadAll is now deprecated. Use io.ReadAll instead.

package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"io"
	"net/http"
	"os"
)

func main() {
	for _, url := range os.Args[1:] {
		resp, err := http.Get(url)
		if err != nil {
			fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "fetch: %v\n", err)
			os.Exit(1)
		}
		b, err := io.ReadAll(resp.Body)
		resp.Body.Close()
		if err != nil {
			fmt.Fprintf(os.Stderr, "fetch: reading %s: %v\n", url, err)
			os.Exit(1)
		}
		fmt.Printf("%s", b)
	}
}
entry.summary: One thing to add before we proceed, While doing the exercises in the book (1.3), I found out that ioutil.Discard has been deprecated, and now it is just io.Discard

entry.title: Learning Go, Day 2
entry.content:
Hello again,

Continuing from where we left off.


Let’s start with Chapter 1.2

1.2 Command-Line Arguments

The os package. The command line args are available to any program in a Variable named Args. os.Args is a slice of strings. Can be accessed the same way as in Python.

os.Args[0] is the name of the command.

I mean, if you look at the syntax. Shit is pretty similar to Python ngl.
Here.

package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"os"
)

func main() {
	var s, sep string
	for i := 0; i < len(os.Args); i++ {
		s += sep + os.Args[i]
		sep = "\n"
	}
	fmt.Println(s)
}

It’s kinda weird we don’t have any parentheses around the for loop tho.

Also, using blank identifiers _ is best practices. Like here:

package main

import (
	"fmt"
	"os"
)

func main() {
	s, sep := "", ""
	for _, arg := range os.Args[0:] {
		s += sep + arg
		sep = " "
	}
	fmt.Println(s)
}

Every s+= results in a creation of a new string. The garbage collector takes care of the ones no longer necessary.

When dealing with large data, a simpler and more efficient solution would be to use strings.Join from the strings package.

entry.title: Learning Go, Day 1
entry.content:
Hi There!

Hope you’re doing well.

I am also following the book “The Go Programming Language” by Alan A. A. Donovan & Brian W. Kernighan.
I will be using this book as a reference to learn Go. If you’re a uni student, you can refer to this book for free from the Oreilly Learning Platform.


What I got to know so far:

1) It has garbage collection, a package system, first-class functions, lexical scope, a syscall interface, and immutable strings in text.

2) It has no implicit numerical conversions, no constructors, no destructors either. No operator overloading? No default parameter values? no inheritance? no generics? no exceptions? no macros? no function annotations? no thread-local storage? WTF?! Looks like I got to unlearn a lot of things (🤓☝️)

3) It is backwards compatible, mature and stable.

4) Go is good for concurrent programming, and it has a built-in concurrency model called goroutines.


With that, Let’s start with Chapter Numero Uno. :)

1.1 Hello, World!

Pretty self explanatory, right? Here’s how you do simple string output in Go:

package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
	fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
}


Go is a compiled language. It is Go toolchain’s job to convert a source program and the things it depends on into instructions in the native machine language.
Another interesting thing is, it natively handles Unicode characters. So, you can use any character from any language in your code.
Say for instance, you save this code as hello.go, you can run it using:

$ go build hello.go
$ ./hello

or simply:

$ go run hello.go

The book then goes into detail about the program itself. About how Go code is organized into packages, which are similar to libraries or modules in other languages. The main package is special, as it defines a standalone executable program, not a library. Each source file begins with a package declaration, which states which package the file belongs to. The main package is the entry point of the program. Importing a package is done using the import keyword. The fmt package is one of the standard library packages that come with Go. It provides functions for formatting text and reading input.

The program will not compile if there are any unused imports or variables. This is to ensure that the code is clean and maintainable.

Go takes formatting seriously. The gofmt tool automatically formats Go code according to a set of rules. This ensures that all Go code looks the same, no matter who wrote it. This is a good thing, as it makes it easier to read and understand code written by others. It is best practice to run gofmt on your code before committing it to version control.


That’s it for today. I will be back with more tomorrow.

Cheers,
Manav

entry.title: Learning Go, Day 0
entry.content:
Hi There!

package main
import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
}

This is how you do simple string output in Go.

I will be following the Official Website, and, down the road, some other online resources I find useful.
You can use the link above to install everything you need to get started with Go.

I will try my best to document my learning journey here.

Cheers!
Manav
